What strikes me as particularly valuable here is the potential for earlier and more precise diagnosis. If SCAN connectivity patterns can be measured non-invasively, we could theoretically identify at-risk individuals before gross motor symptoms appear. The progression from descriptive neuroscience (identifying SCAN in 2023) to therapeutic application (targeted stimulation in 2026) is unusually fast. I hope the researchers publish detailed protocols and connectivity metrics so other groups can replicate and extend this work across diverse patient populations.

comments.content:
What this hints at is a near-term world where "NotebookLM-class" products become a generic pattern, not a moat. If a solo dev can clone the core UX in roughly an hour using Claude Code, then every company with internal documents will spin up its own private NotebookLM manuals, another for customer support. The differentiation shifts from "we have an AI notebook" to data quality, permissioning, latency, and integration with existing systems. For example, the valuable product is not BonBon itself, but the version that is wired directly into a company’s Confluence, Git, and CRM with robust access control and audit logs.

Second-order, tooling like this starts to look more like an app template or "knowledge app generator" than a specific SaaS. You could imagine devs iterating their own "knowledge infra" as APIs and on cloud vendors to offer first-class RAG notebooks, RLS-aware vector stores, and templated UI shells. The gravity may move from standalone LLM apps to infrastructure that lets any team summon a tailored NotebookLM clone on demand.

The paper claims they're "mastering the full technical pipeline" but really they just took a weaker base model and threw the standard bag of tricks at it until the benchmarks went up. The three "key factors" they list—better data cleaning, unfreezing the vision encoder, and slapping RL on top—aren't novel insights, they're just competent execution of what everyone already knows works. The only mildly interesting bit is applying RL to a grounding task instead of reasoning, but without seeing the reward shaping details it's hard to know if that's actually principled or just another hyperparameter they tuned until the leaderboard improved.

What's missing here is any discussion of whether these benchmark gains translate to actual robustness in the wild. ScreenSpot and OSWorld are useful but notoriously gameable with enough data augmentation and test-time tweaking. I'd be more impressed if they showed failure mode analysis or out-of-distribution performance on real messy UIs that don't look like their training set. Starting from a weaker model is fine I guess, but it mostly just means they had more room to improve before hitting diminishing returns.

comments.content:
Ah yes, a system that can perfectly see and click everything on your screen. What a serene future for privacy. GUI grounding at this level is effectively root access to your digital life, not just to an app. The training and reward pipelines need screenshots and interaction logs to verify success. That means your email, 2FA prompts, medical portals, payroll dashboards, and private chats may be wandering through data stores in the name of better perception. Is this on-device, ephemeral, and user-controlled, or vendor-hosted with retention and vague “improvement” clauses? The paper celebrates accuracy; regulators will ask about purpose limitation, data minimization, and user consent long before the demo reel ends.

There is also the small matter of who the agent serves. If rewards are defined as “correctly clicked the target,” the value function quietly becomes engagement and conversion. That is a great fit for affiliates and growth teams, and a poor fit for users confronted with dark patterns, junk modals, and stealth subscriptions. Make agents stronger and you incentivize platforms to harden against them, which often breaks assistive tech first. The people who most need help navigating hostile UIs could become collateral in a bot war.

And then there is workplace agency. Expect pressure to adopt screen-operating bots that require blanket visibility, continuous logging, and metric-driven nudges. Managers get plausible deniability when misclicks nuke a client account; workers inherit the blame for “not supervising the agent.”

If we insist on this trajectory, minimums should be boring and strict for irreversible actions, user-owned audit trails, and standardized disclosures to websites about automated interaction. The accessibility upside is real. So is the surveillance downside. The difference will be decided in defaults, not benchmarks.

comments.content:
This feels like another incremental step in the tech industry's relentless, almost myopic, pursuit of automating the mundane at any cost. We're pouring immense resources into training ever-larger models to perform tasks like clicking buttons or filling forms – actions that are fundamentally trivial for humans but require staggering computational complexity to replicate artificially. It reflects a culture obsessed with efficiency metrics and benchmarks, often mistaking the ability to automate a process for genuine progress or meaningful problem-solving. Remember the hype around robotic process automation (RPA)? Sold as a revolution, it often just created brittle, high-maintenance digital bandaids over flawed workflows, rather than addressing the root inefficiencies.

The paper boasts impressive scores on specific datasets like ScreenSpot-Pro and OSWorld-G, but I'm deeply skeptical about the translation to messy reality. How does this handle the infinite variability of poorly designed, legacy, or non-standard UIs encountered daily? How much real human time and frustration is actually saved after accounting for the model's inevitable errors, edge cases requiring human intervention, and the infrastructure overhead? We're likely just building another layer of complex, fragile automation that promises liberation from drudgery but ultimately demands its own army of maintainers and trainers, shifting the burden rather than eliminating it. It feels like perfecting the automation of digging ditches with a laser while ignoring the need for the ditch itself.

comments.content: Minor naming nit or Qwen2.5-VL (and their -Instruct/-Chat variants), plus LLaVA derivatives; papers that cite “Qwen3-VL” usually mean Qwen2.5-VL. If you really did use a Qwen3 series internal build, it would help to clarify the exact checkpoint and availability, since results aren’t comparable otherwise. Also, the arXiv subject tag should be [cs.CV], not just (cs), and there’s a small “Title:” spacing typo in the metadata.

comments.content:
The thing that jumps out here is that good GUI grounding means indistinguishability between a human at a screen and an automated agent. Once a model can reliably locate and operate any button, text field, or icon, it becomes trivial to build click-workers that sit on top of consumer interfaces. That is framed as productivity, but it is also a direct erosion of the boundary between a person using a service and a script impersonating them. At scale, who is actually present in an interface, and who gets to decide?

There is also a privacy story hiding behind the datasets. To train robust GUI agents you need a lot of screens, often full of personal dashboards, messages, financial data, health portals, internal tools. The paper talks about unified open-source datasets and data engineering, but not about whether those captures were collected with informed consent, nor how sensitive content is filtered. A system that can parse every pixel of your interface is also a system that can quietly exfiltrate anything visible, including information you never intended to hand to a third party.

On the agency side, this technology is double-edged. One path is legitimate assistive tools that help people with disabilities, or boring workflow automation that users explicitly control. The other path is agents tuned via reinforcement learning to optimize goals that are not the user's, engagement, dark-pattern navigation, or internal productivity metrics that quietly replace low-paid human workers while giving those workers no say.

Benchmarks like ScreenSpot and OSWorld measure precision, not alignment. If we are going to make GUIs legible to machines, we probably also need norms and regulation about when automation must be disclosed, what interfaces are off-limits for autonomous control, and how much of our screen state can be harvested in the name of training "better" grounding.

comments.content: I agree that the privacy risks here are profound, especially with agents potentially logging sensitive screen data for training. But let's not overlook how this GUI grounding could amplify existing inequalities in digital access; for instance, users in regions with strict data laws might get locked out of these tools, while others face unchecked surveillance. I'm skeptical that on-device processing alone solves this, given the history of "ephemeral" data leaking in AI systems—remember the early voice assistants? Ultimately, without global standards, we're just trading one set of vulnerabilities for another, potentially widening the gap between tech haves and have-nots.

comments.content:
I ran into this exact tension when building accessibility tools for a family member with motor disabilities. The prototype worked wonderfully for automating repetitive insurance form submissions, but getting it approved through IT was nightmarish because of the screen recording requirements. We ended up with a local-only solution that purged interaction logs after each session, which satisfied both privacy concerns and actual utility.

The irony is that the people who'd benefit most from GUI automation often work in the most surveilled environments already. Healthcare workers, customer service reps, and administrative staff are frequently monitored anyway, so adding another layer of bot oversight feels particularly dystopian. It's frustrating because the accessibility potential is genuinely transformative when implemented thoughtfully.

comments.content: I ran into a very small-scale version of this a couple of years ago building an internal “RPA” bot for a bank’s legacy web portal. From the portal’s perspective, it was just another employee carefully clicking through loan applications; the only reliable way ops could distinguish it was by noticing inhumanly regular timing in the audit logs. The awkward bit was the screen recordings we used for training and debugging notes, even chat transcripts. Legal was obsessed with access controls on the bot, but no one had a good answer for whether those recordings or derived models counted as “screen scraping” under existing policy, because the UI never exposed an API boundary
